coredata.dc:title: Carbon Dot-Modified ferrite (CDs@ZF): Efficient Visible-Light photocatalyst for dye degradation and antibacterial activity

coredata.dc:description: To effectively address the detrimental rhodamine (RhB) and methylene blue (MB) dyes in wastewater, zinc ferrite (ZF) has emerged as a promising material for promoting photocatalytic breakdown processes. Experiments were conducted under varying conditions of pH (2–12), catalyst dosage (0.25–2.0 g/L), and initial dye concentrations (5–200 mg/L). In this study, CDs-decorated ZF microspheres were synthesized and successfully used to degrade RhB and MB dyes under visible light. The CDs@ZF nanocomposites demonstrated significantly better performance than pure ZF, achieving nearly 94.25% photocatalytic breakdown of MB in 70 min and 96.1 % in the case of Rh-B within 60 min. Furthermore, the CD@ZF material exhibited stability after five consecutive photocatalytic cycles. The photocatalytic mechanism involves either the direct charge transfer by the generation of photogenerated carriers(holes) or the reactive species like hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and superoxide radicals (•O2). These findings indicate that CDs@ZF nanocomposites are capable of being effective candidates for effluent treatment and have potential applications in various other fields. Additionally, the engineered photocatalyst was studied for its antibacterial effectiveness against both Gram-positive(S. aureus)and Gram-negative(P. vulgaris)bacteria. It employs visible light to solve environmental issues positively.

head.ce:title: Carbon Dot-Modified ferrite (CDs@ZF): Efficient Visible-Light photocatalyst for dye degradation and antibacterial activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Srinagar Hazratbal, Srinagar, Jammu and Kashmir 190006, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Srinagar Hazratbal, Srinagar, Jammu and Kashmir 190006, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To effectively address the detrimental rhodamine (RhB) and methylene blue (MB) dyes in wastewater, zinc ferrite (ZF) has emerged as a promising material for promoting photocatalytic breakdown processes. Experiments were conducted under varying conditions of pH (2–12), catalyst dosage (0.25–2.0 g/L), and initial dye concentrations (5–200 mg/L). In this study, CDs-decorated ZF microspheres were synthesized and successfully used to degrade RhB and MB dyes under visible light. The CDs@ZF nanocomposites demonstrated significantly better performance than pure ZF, achieving nearly 94.25% photocatalytic breakdown of MB in 70 min and 96.1 % in the case of Rh-B within 60 min. Furthermore, the CD@ZF material exhibited stability after five consecutive photocatalytic cycles. The photocatalytic mechanism involves either the direct charge transfer by the generation of photogenerated carriers(holes) or the reactive species like hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and superoxide radicals (•O2). These findings indicate that CDs@ZF nanocomposites are capable of being effective candidates for effluent treatment and have potential applications in various other fields. Additionally, the engineered photocatalyst was studied for its antibacterial effectiveness against both Gram-positive(S. aureus)and Gram-negative(P. vulgaris)bacteria. It employs visible light to solve environmental issues positively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dyes are widely applied in the global textile, printing, and leather industries. Many different colors may be found in the wastewater produced by such industries. Contamination of drinking water to an extreme degree has adverse effects on human beings, other creatures, and the natural world. As a result of their complexity, they are notoriously hard to degrade. During the dying process, the vast majority of them are never linked to the fibers, and as a result, they are released into the environment[1]. These chemically stable, non-biodegradable, and possibly permanent dyes pose severe concerns to human and environmental health as their usage continues to rise. Adsorption, biotreatments, nano-filtration, centrifugation, and activated sludge method, are a few techniques that may be used to remove dyestuffs from water[2]. Yet, these approaches have not seen widespread application due to their high price, low competence, and sluggish progress[3,4].

Recently, the photocatalytic process has garnered significant attention as an effective technique for removing contaminants from wastewater[5]. This growing interest is driven by the method's affordability, efficiency, and promising potential to degrade dyes found in textile effluents. In photocatalysis, a photocatalyst—typically a metal oxide, metal sulfide, or ferrite in its pristine, doped, or composite form—utilizes UV or visible light to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), which break down dyes into their basic constituents.[6]semiconductor materials like TiO2, ZnO, and SnO2 were employed as catalysts for dye degradation; however, their wide bandgap restricted their activity to UV light, which constitutes less than 5 % of the solar spectrum, making them less effective for photocatalytic applications[7]. In subsequent years, researchers identified metal oxides, metal sulfides, and ferrite materials with visible light-active bandgaps as more suitable alternatives, as these materials can utilize visible light, accounting for approximately 43% of the solar spectrum[8]. Additionally, advancements such as doping, co-doping, heterojunction formation, and composite fabrication, have been implemented to enhance the light-harvesting capabilities and overall performance of photocatalysts[9].

Scientists are particularly intrigued by spinel ferrites (MFe2O4) due to their versatile applications across technology and industry. These materials have been utilized in the development of catalysts, water purification systems, dye degradation agents, permanent magnets, drug delivery systems, microwave devices, and more. Among ferrite materials, spinel ferrites are the most widely used, and their application as photocatalysts for water treatment is highly cost-effective, owing to their natural abundance[3,10].

Carbon nanodots, distinct carbon-based nanoparticles spherical in shape, featuring diameters smaller than 10 nm, have attracted significant attention due to their affordability, non-toxicity, robustness to thermal and chemical changes, and straightforward synthesis[11]. CQDs exhibit exceptional electron transfer and reservoir capabilities because of their conjugated framework[12]. The up-converted photoluminescence (PL) feature of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) allows them to absorb lower-energy photons (near-infrared region) and re-emit them as higher-energy photons(visible light or near-infrared region)[13,14]. This property effectively extends the absorption range of wide-bandgap semiconductors, enabling them to capture more light beyond their natural absorption limits. It also increases their property of exciton dissociation thus improving their property of photocatalysis. CDs have been adeptly assimilated into a range of semiconductors[13,15], including polymers like graphitic carbon nitride[15,16], metal oxides, and ternary sulfides[17,18]. Imperfections that lead to the recombination of photo-excited electron-hole pairs often make it difficult to achieve an ideal interface in larger materials[19]. Nonetheless, the tiny size of carbon dots and the profusion of functional groups at the surface reduces interfacial mismatches with other semiconductors, creating a distinct interface that improves charge transfer and boosts photocatalytic activity[20,21].

In this study, the combination of carbon dots (CDs) with zinc ferrite (ZF) significantly improves the overall efficiency of photocatalysis in degrading synthetic dyes as exemplified by methylene blue (MB) and rhodamine B (RhB) compared to using the catalysts individually. This study presents the synthesis of a novel carbon dots (CDs)-modified zinc ferrite (ZF) nanoparticle, accompanied by detailed analyses of its morphology, structure, optical properties, and photocatalytic capabilities. The photocatalytic activity of the CDs@ZF samples was assessed through the degradation of methylene blue (MB) and rhodamine B (RhB) dyes. CDs@ZF results in enhanced photocatalytic activity due to the combined effects of optimized light capture and the enhanced separation of the exciton. Additionally, the antibacterial activity was studied against both Gram-negative bacteria and Gram-positive bacterial strains by determining the inhibition zone.

An effective photocatalyst must exhibit excellent reusability, a high surface-area-to-volume ratio, and a reduced bandgap[22]. Ferrite materials, known for their stability and reusability, were chosen for photocatalytic applications. Their magnetic properties enable rapid and simple separation after dye degradation. Nanotechnology was employed to increase the specific surface area of the catalyst, improving its overall performance[23]. By modifying nanoscale properties, ferrites become more efficient, making them ideal for catalysis. Nanomaterials excel in catalytic processes due to their high surface-area-to-volume ratio and are widely used in fields like energy storage and photonics[23,24]. To further enhance photocatalysis, a composite of carbon dots (CDs) and zinc ferrite was synthesized. CDs offer tunable band gaps, high surface-area-to-volume ratios, and adaptable pore sizes, boosting light absorption and reactant interaction[25]. Zinc ferrite provides magnetic stability and stands out as a highly promising inorganic semiconductor due to its narrow band gap, ranging from 2.2 to 1.7 ev[26]. While the composite improves charge separation and broad-spectrum light absorption, this innovative material accelerates pollutant degradation and has potential in wastewater treatment and clean energy.

Zn(NO3)2•6 H2O(99 %), Fe(NO3)2•6 H2O(98 %), isopropanol(99.5 %), 1,2 propanediol, Methylene blue (>96 %), Rhodamine B ((≥95 %), and ethanol (99.9 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and Merck Co. and were utilized as received without further purification.

The Perkin-Elmer FT-IR spectrophotometer (KBr disks, 400–4000 cm−1) was employed to acquire the FT-IR spectrum. The Rigaku Smart Lab XRD was used to record XRD data. The ZEISS Gemini SEM 500 FE-SEM and Octane Elect EDS are used for the SEM and EDAX analysis. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was employed to visualize and characterize the morphology of the catalysts. Utilizing the Perkin Elmer Lambda 365 UV–Visible Spectrophotometer, DR-UV investigations were determined. Zeta potential was carried out using Particle Analyzer (LitesizerTM 500). XPS analysis was performed using a Thermoscientific NEXA Surface Analyzer. The photoelectrochemical capacity of the materials was assessed using an electrochemical workstation (Bio-Logic SP-200). The nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm was investigated using a Quanta chrome NOVA 4200 analyzer operating at 77K. Each sample was degassed at 200°C overnight. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis technique has determined the surface area.

To synthesize carbon quantum dots (CDs), freshly harvested Ganoderma applanatum mushrooms were initially dried in an oven set to 60°C for two days. After drying, the mushrooms are then pulverized with an electric grinder and subsequently sieved by passing through a standard 200-mesh sieve to obtain fine particles. The carbon dots derived from Ganoderma applanatum mushrooms (LCDs) were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal process.200ml of deionized water (DI water) were mixed with 6g of Ganoderma applanatum mushroom as part of a standard procedure. After that, the mixture was sonicated for 30min using an Ultra-Sonicator Bath. The mixture was rinsed with 100ml of deionized water (DI water) after being sonicated and then moved to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel hydrothermal autoclave reactor. For 12h, the reactor containing the mixture was kept in an oven set to 200°C. When the heating process was finished, the reactor was cooled and opened, revealing a solid substance suspended in a yellow liquid. To eliminate larger particles, the solution was first separated using paper filtration. To further get rid of any last big particles, centrifugation was done for 20min at 11,000rpm. Dialysis was performed on the resultant solution for 24h in deionized water using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1kDa. Ultimately, a powder (LCD) was obtained by freeze-drying the dialyzed solution [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon Dots-Zinc Ferriate nanocomposite(CD@ZF)
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrothermal synthesis process entails dissolving a 1:2 ratio of Zn2+ (from Zn(NO3)2·6H2O) and Fe3+ (from Fe(NO3)3·9H2O) in water. Following this, ethanol is slowly added drop by drop with continuous stirring to ensure thorough mixing of the solution. The resultant blend is subsequently introduced into an autoclave and subjected to elevated pressure and temperature to dissolve and reform the precursor(s). The dark brown paste obtained was combined with the previously synthesized yellowish carbon dot solution and placed into a 100 ml Teflon-lined autoclave, then heated to 180°C for 8h. Afterward, the autoclave was permitted to cool to ambient temperature. Finally, the sample underwent three rinsing cycles with deionized water, followed by one cycle with ethanol, and was then dried overnight at 70°C. Following drying, the sample underwent grinding for 30 min before being subjected to calcination at 800°C for 12h, afterward cooling naturally to ambient conditions[28]. The process of synthesizing composites is illustrated in scheme 1 . Additionally, basic ZF nanoparticles were produced separately for comparison.

The composite's photocatalytic ability was tested by decomposing methyl blue (MB) and rhodamine-B (RH-B) under visible light irradiation from a 200 W Xenon lamp at room temperature. At room temperature, 50 mL of the reaction solution containing 10 mg/L catalyst and dye (10 mg/L in neutral condition) was put in a 100 mL beaker and agitated with a magnetron. Before illumination, the combined solution was gently stirred for an hour in the dark to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium on the catalyst's surface. During the photocatalytic study, a tiny sample of the reaction solution was taken out at regular intervals to evaluate dye concentration, which was calculated by determining the solution's absorbance at a set wavelength using an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer. The reaction solution was centrifuged for 10 min to remove the catalyst at 4000 rmin−1 before measuring the absorbance. To evaluate the photocatalytic stability of the CD@ZF nanocomposite material, recycling studies for dye degradation employing the composites were carried out. After the first cycle, the photocatalyst was centrifuged, rinsed with water, and dried. The recovered photocatalyst was added to a new dye solution for the second cycle of the photocatalytic experiment, which was carried out under identical circumstances[4]. The degradation rate (D) is computed using the following equation:
(1)

100 %
Co is the preliminary concentration, and Ct is the concentration at time intervals.

A Bio-Logic SP-200 electrochemical station was used to assess the photoelectrochemical capability of nanocomposite. A typical three-electrode setup was used for electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The working electrode, plated on ITO, had an active area of 1 cm2. The reference electrode was an Ag/AgCl electrode, while the counter electrode was a Pt wire. A solution of 0.5 M NaOH served as the electrolyte. An open-circuit voltage bias was provided at a frequency of 0.4 Hz and a current amplitude of 10 mV. Using the same configuration, the Mott-Schottky plots were generated from the same workstation.

The antibacterial properties of CD@ZF against P. vulgaris and S. aureus were investigated using the agar-well diffusion technique, as described in earlier publications. Overnight cultures of the desired bacteria (50 μl) were distributed on Mueller Hinton (MH) agar matrix containing beef extract, acid casein hydrolysate, starch, and agar. Agar was perforated with a cork borer to make wells measuring 6 mm in diameter. 30 μl of the investigated drugs were added. The plates were chilled at 4 °C for two hours to allow the ingredients to permeate into the medium. After that, the plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, an inspection was performed, and photos of bacterial inhibitions were captured.

To investigate the crystalline framework and phase purity of ZF and CDs@ZF, X-ray diffraction (XRD) was utilized. The diffraction peaks seen in every sample, as shown in Fig. 1 a, verify the existence of ZF, a sphere-shaped spinel. In the ZF XRD pattern (JCPDS No. 00–022–1012), the peaks at 2θ values of 29.51°, 34.96°, 42.53°, 53.3°, 56.23°, and 61.19°, respectively, represent reflections from the (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) crystallographic planes[29]. Despite the lack of a characteristic CD peak (approximately 23°) in the CDs/ZF sample, this can be explained by the synthesized material's low CD content[4,30,31]. Furthermore, the Debye-Scherrer formula was used to calculate the average crystallite sizes as follows:
(2)
D =
K
λ
β cos θ
Where λ is the X-ray wavelength (0.154 nm), β is the full-width at half maximum (FWHM), θ is the incident angle of the X-ray, D is the crystallite size, and K is the shape factor (having a value of 0.9). The percentage crystallinity, percentage porosity, and specific surface area of ZF and CD@ZF nanomaterials were determined using equations (3),(4), and (5), respectively[32–34]. Table S1 presents the data on crystalline size, diffraction peak position, percentage crystallinity, percentage porosity, and specific surface area

FTIR is a vital technique for analyzing the chemical environment and identifying the functional groups present in a nanocomposite. The surface functional groups of C-dots were examined using FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 1b). The broad, intense band at 3384 cm−1 indicates the asymmetric stretching vibrations of –OH and N–H groups[35]. The intense band at 1619 cm−1 is attributed to the amide bond (O = C–NH), in agreement with earlier studies. This band may also be attributed to the C = O stretching vibration at 1665 cm−1 and the N–H bending vibration at 1600 cm−1 [35,36]. Based on these observations and comparison with the literature, it is concluded that the C-dots contain multiple hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl functional groups. The spectrum of ZF shows peaks at approximately 421, 564, and 3848 cm−1. The broad peak at 3848 cm−1 is attributed to adsorbed water's bending and stretching vibrations. The two bands at 421 cm−1 and 565 cm−1 are associated with the vibrations of metal–oxygen bonds, specifically Fe–O and Zn–O, at six coordination site(octahedral) and four coordination site (tetrahedral) sites, respectively[37,38]. Additionally, after the incorporation of CDs, new functional groups appeared. In the FTIR spectrum of CD@ZF, additional weak bands emerged at 683 cm−1, indicating the presence of the Fe–O group[35,39].

SEM and HR-TEM images were used to analyze the general morphologies of the CDs, ZF, and CDs@ZF nanocomposite. ImageJ software was used to estimate the size distribution. Fig. 2 a and 2b show representative HR-TEM and SEM images of CDs, respectively. The synthesized C-dots' HRTEM image, displayed in Fig. 2a, emphasizes their spherical shape and minuscule size. The CD nanoparticles are monodisperse, with a uniform spherical morphology. This mono dispersity facilitates their interaction with other materials to form nanocomposites. The spherical shape of the CDs also enhances their surface area. Furthermore, the CDs are well-dispersed in solution, which offers significant potential for aqueous reactions, particularly in photocatalytic applications. The corresponding size distribution histogram, with CD sizes ranging from 0.1 to 3.0 nm, is displayed in the inset of Fig. 2a. Based on the HRTEM image, the average size of the CDs is calculated to be 1.61 nm[40,41].

The ZF nanoparticles' HRTEM image (Fig. 3 b) shows a closely packed configuration of nanoparticles, most of which have a spherical shape. It is estimated that the ZF nanoparticles have an average size of 19 nm (Fig. 3b). The polycrystalline nature of the ZF nanoparticles is further confirmed by the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern shown in Fig. 3c. The ZF nanoparticles' approximately spherical shape is also shown by the SEM image (Fig. 3a). Fig. 3e shows a representative HRTEM image of the magnetic nanocomposites, revealing that CDs are encapsulated within the porous ZF, likely due to the agglomeration and uniform distribution of ZF on the CD surface[42]. The nanocomposite size, based on the HRTEM image inset in Fig. 3e, has an average size of 25.51 nm. The SAED pattern for the magnetic nanocomposites (Fig. 3f) displays a ring pattern from the spherical ZF and spot patterns from the CDs, further confirming the successful formation of CD-ZF hybrids[43].

To confirm the loading of CDs onto the ZF structure, elemental mapping through energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy was performed, with the results shown in Fig. 4 . The elemental mapping confirmed the presence of C, N, O, Fe, and Zn, indicating the coexistence of both CDs and ZF phases. Furthermore, the elements were uniformly distributed across the CDs@ZF nanocomposite, as demonstrated by the elemental overlap image in Fig. 4(a). The EDX spectra also verified the successful loading of CDs onto ZF nanoparticles, with a quantitative composition of 21.5 % C, 5.4 % N, 27.4 % O, 23.5 % Fe, and 22.2 % Zn, as illustrated in Fig. 4(g). The FESEM analysis confirms the uniform distribution of CDs on the surface of ZF nanoparticles.

The elemental makeup and metallic state of the CD@ZF nanocomposite were examined using XPS analysis. The elements Zn, Fe, O, C, and N were verified to be present in the material by the survey spectra (Fig. 5 (a)). Fig. 5(b–f) displays the deconvoluted XPS spectra for O (1s), C (1s), N (1s), Fe (2p), and Zn (2p), in that order. The presence of divalent Zn2+ species at tetrahedral sites is confirmed for Zn by two peaks at 1022.94 eV and 1045.86 eV, which correspond to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 (Fig. 5(f))[44,45]. Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 are represented by the two main peaks at 710.49 eV and 724.93 eV in the high-resolution spectra for Fe (2p) (Fig. 5(e)), as well as a satellite peak at 721.35 eV. The presence of Fe3+ at octahedral sites is indicated by the Fe 2p3/2 peak's further deconvolution into two spin states at 712.35 eV and 710.49 eV[38,46]. This demonstrates that, in accordance with a typical spinel structure, Zn2+ occupies four coordinated positions (tetrahedral sites) and Fe3+ occupies six coordinated positions (octahedral sites). Four peaks were found at 529.9 eV, 530.69 eV, 531.34 eV, and 532.67 eV in the O (1s) spectra (Fig. 5(b). These peaks represent surface hydroxyl groups, Fe–O bonds, Fe–C–O bonds, and oxygen from the crystal lattice[42,47]. These findings imply that the Fe and C groups in the CD@ZF nanocomposite have strong interactions and bonds. The inclusion of carbon dots (CDs) is confirmed by the presence of C (1 s) and N (1 s) signals in the XPS spectrum of CD@ZF, which is displayed in Fig. 4(c) and (d)[48,49]. The presence of CDs is further confirmed by the high-resolution C (1 s) spectrum, which shows three fitted peaks that correspond to sp3-hybridized C–C bonds at 284.4 eV, C–N bonds at 285.2 eV, and C = O/C = N bonds at 286.9 eV. At 398.21 eV, 399.98 eV, and 401.82 eV, the N (1s) spectrum deconvolutes into three broad peaks that represent pyridinic, pyrrolic, and tertiary nitrogen species, respectively[50].

The surface area of a material is a critical factor in evaluating its effectiveness as a photocatalyst. The specific surface area (S_BET) of the synthesized CD@ZF catalyst was determined using the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm and the BET method. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the catalyst has a surface area of 67.756 m2/g, which enhances its interaction with the surrounding environment. The catalyst exhibits an average pore diameter of 3.037 nm and a total pore volume of 0.037 cc/g. The type IV adsorption–desorption isotherm shown in Fig. 6a highlights the efficient adsorption properties of imine, hydroxyl, amino groups, and cobalt ferrite nanoparticles. The CD@ZF catalyst exhibits a slit-like morphology, as illustrated in Fig. 6b. The hysteresis loop observed in the 0.8–1.0 relative pressure range is shifted toward a higher P/Po region, indicating the mesoporous nature of the material. The isotherm shows a progression from monolayer adsorption to multilayer adsorption, and eventually capillary condensation. The shift to the right is due to the presence of larger pores, which require higher pressure for filling, as multilayer formation occurs on low-energy sites further from the adsorbent[51,52].

The magnetic properties of ZF and the CD@ZF nanocomposite were analyzed using a Lakshore VSM 7401 vibrating sample magnetometer. The saturation magnetization (Ms) of ZF and CD@ZF was measured across a wide range of magnetic field strengths at room temperature. The magnetization curves presented in Fig. 7 show that ZF has a saturation magnetization of 27 emu/g, while CD@ZF exhibits a lower value of 20 emu/g. The decrease in saturation magnetization for the CD@ZF nanocomposite compared to ZF can be due to factors such as differences in particle size, concentration, and the presence of the non-magnetic CD component. Despite the reduced magnetization, CD@ZF still exhibits magnetic behavior akin to superparamagnetic materials, allowing it to be easily isolated from aqueous solutions using an external magnetic field. This experimental data suggests that the synthesized nanocomposite not only has good catalytic properties but can also be efficiently separated and recycled using magnetic methods[53].

The optical properties of the material play a vital role in photocatalysis, as they help assess the bandgap and light absorption characteristics. UV–vis diffuse reflectance measurements were conducted to evaluate how effectively different samples absorbed UV and visible light, as illustrated in Fig. 8 (a). The band at 310 nm is linked to the n → π* transition, while the band at 229 nm is attributed to the π → π* transition. A noticeable redshift of these bands in CD@ZF, to 238 nm and 326 nm, is shown in Fig. 8(a). The photocatalyst's band gap energy (Eg) was estimated using the Kubelka-Munk function, with Tauc plots for ZF NPs and CD@ZF presented in Fig. 8(b) and (c) respectively. The two samples showed band gap energies of 2.11 eV and 1.95 eV, respectively[54]. The significant rise in band gap energy for CD@ZF is ascribed to electron migration between the conduction and valence bands. This hinders the electron-hole recombination process, making it more difficult and time-consuming, which in turn enhances the photocatalyst's degradation efficiency[55].

The solution pH is a critical factor in the photodegradation of dyes. Fig. 9 (b) shows how pH influences the degradation of Methyl Blue (MB) and Rhodamine B (RH-B) dyes using the CD@ZF catalyst. The optimal pH for MB degradation was found to be 7, achieving a 92.5 % degradation rate, while for RH-B, the optimal pH was 9, with a degradation rate of 90.1 %. At other pH values (2, 4, 6, 9, and 11), the degradation rates for both dyes decreased, highlighting the importance of pH in the degradation process. The point of zero charge (PZC) of the CD@ZF catalyst is 7.6, with a zeta potential of −29 mV around pH 9. As a result, the catalyst surface becomes protonated at neutral pH, carrying a net positive charge. MB, a cationic dye containing an N–H group, interacts strongly with the negatively charged catalyst surface, resulting in effective electrostatic interactions that favor MB degradation at neutral pH. In contrast, at alkaline pH, the catalyst surface becomes negatively charged, making it more effective at degrading cationic dyes like RH-B. At pH 9, the catalyst surface achieves maximum negative charge, enhancing its interaction with RH-B through strong electrostatic attraction. The zeta potential analysis in Fig. 9(a) further supports this, showing that the catalyst's surface is positively charged in acidic and neutral environments and negatively charged in alkaline conditions. At pH values below 3 or above 9, the catalyst and dye molecules repel each other, reducing adsorption and lowering photocatalytic efficiency. Based on these findings, the highest dye degradation was observed at pH 7 for MB and pH 9 for RH-B[56].

In addition to pH, the catalyst's dosage is an important factor, as optimizing it helps reduce production costs and minimize waste. Fig. 9(c) shows the effect of varying the amount of catalyst on dye degradation, without altering the pH of the dye solution. The degradation rate increases as the catalyst dosage increases, up to 1 g/L, which is expected since a higher amount of catalyst provides more active sites and produces more reactive species. However, when the dosage exceeds 1 g/L, the degradation rate decreases. This reduction may be due to the solution becoming opaque with excess catalyst, which impairs light absorption and slows the degradation process. Therefore, the optimal catalyst concentration is 1 g/L.

To study the effectiveness of catalysts in wastewater remediation, synthetic dyes like RhB and MB dyes, which are found in fabric waste, are typically used as reference pollutants. The marked absorption peaks of MB at 664 nm and RhB at 553 nm have been monitored over time under irradiation with CD@ZF in order to track the degradation of these dyes through photocatalysis (Fig. 10 d and 10a). Without a shift in the absorption peak toward the blue region, the RhB spectrum evolution (Fig. 10a) demonstrates a decrease in absorbance. According to earlier research, ●OH radicals in bulk solutions mainly attack the chromophore ring, which breaks down the structure of Rhodamine B causing the reduction in absorbance without altering wavelength[57,58]. De-ethylation of the aromatic rings, which produces notable blue shifts, is another possible mechanism. Our results validate that the previously described mechanism is responsible for RhB degradation. Furthermore, the color fading of MB and RhB indicates that they were nearly completely decomposed, indicating that these organic dyes were decomposed. The CD@ZF nanocomposite shows a lot of promise as a successful photocatalyst for wastewater treatment, according to this analysis. The CD@ZF nanocomposite shows a lot of promise as a successful photocatalyst for wastewater treatment, according to this analysis. Additionally, the photocatalytic degradation rates of RhB and MB were assessed when exposed to visible light. With C standing for the concentration at a specific moment in time and C0 for the initial concentration, Fig. 10b depicts the time-based correlation between the relative concentration of Rhodamine B (C/C0) and elapsed time. Under comparable experimental circumstances, the photocatalytic performance of CD, ZF, and CD@ZF was concurrently compared. To evaluate the combined effects of adsorption, photolysis, and photocatalysis, a thorough controlled procedure on the adsorption-based decolorization of Rhodamine B using photocatalysts in the absence of light, and on its photocatalytic breakdown under UV exposure, both with and without the presence of CD@ZF. Under visible light, the synthesized CD@ZF's adsorption characteristics were examined. After 30 min in the dark, adsorption–desorption equilibrium with Rhodamine B was reached. The highest adsorption decolorization of 13.4 % was observed at equilibrium in CD@ZF, most likely as a result of their larger pore volume and BET surface area. RhB photolysis was negligible in blank tests when exposed to visible light alone (without catalysts), suggesting that it is relatively stable under visible light. Nevertheless, RhB degradation rates dramatically rose when a photocatalyst and visible light were combined, suggesting that photocatalysis is the primary degradation pathway in this investigation. In contrast to ZF and CDs, the CD@ZF demonstrated the highest photocatalytic activity, with RhB degradation approaching 96.1 % within a time period of 60 min. Fig. 4d illustrates the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of methylene blue (MB) under visible light using ZF, CD, and CD@ZF as catalysts. Without a photocatalyst, the direct photodegradation of MB under visible light was minimal, demonstrating MB's strong stability under visible irradiation. Before visible exposure, adsorption/desorption equilibrium was established between the photocatalyst and MB during the photocatalytic process. Notably, CD@ZF exhibited an enhanced affinity for methylene blue compared to pure ZnFe2O4 or CD alone, achieving up to 12.1 % adsorption decolorization. ZF visible irradiation for 30 min caused about 63.1 % of MB to degrade. Interestingly, CD@ZF showed the highest photocatalytic efficiency, degrading MB by 94.25 % under the same conditions. This suggests that ZF's photocatalytic performance is greatly accelerated by the synergistic effect of CDs[59]. These findings unequivocally demonstrate that when exposed to visible light, CD@ZF demonstrates superior photocatalytic activity for breaking down both MB and RhB organic dye. The photodegradation of RhB and MB is described by a pseudo-first-order kinetic model, which is represented as −ln(C0/C) = Kt, where K is the rate constant, as illustrated in Fig. 10c and 10f. With the K values for the degradation of RhB and MB under UV light shown in Table 1 , the CD@ZF showed strong photocatalytic activity[60]. When compared to ZF and other samples, this is noticeably higher. Table 2 contrasts the current binary composite's photocatalytic dye degradation with that of previously published comparable nanocomposite materials[61].

The antibacterial properties of the unmodified composite were evaluated through a series of assays using various Gram-negative (G-) and Gram-positive (G + ) bacterial strains. The results, illustrated in Fig. 11 , are presented as zones of inhibition (ZOI), determined by the well diffusion method. Both synthesized materials, including CD and CD@ZF, were tested for their antibacterial effectiveness and compared to a standard antibiotic (Streptomycin, 0.5 mg) as a positive control, with DMSO used as the negative control. The incorporation of Streptomycin led to circular inhibition zones in both CD and CD@ZF samples, demonstrating growth inhibition in S. aureus (G + ) and P. vulgaris (G-) bacteria, indicating that the synthesized materials possess antibiotic properties. The results suggest that increasing the concentration of the composite correlates with a larger zone of inhibition. The primary mechanism behind the antibacterial activity involves visible light irradiation, which stimulates the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as superoxide, hydroxyl radicals, hydrogen peroxide, and singlet oxygen[68]. These ROS induce oxidative stress, leading to the damage of enzymes, proteins, and DNA, thereby preventing bacterial colony growth. Additionally, the metal ions (Zn2+ or Fe2+) in the composite are attracted to the negatively charged bacterial cell membranes, disrupting the bacteria by inhibiting DNA replication and causing protein denaturation. CD@ZF exhibited the largest ZOI against pathogens compared to the other materials studied, as shown in Table 3 . The enhanced antibacterial performance of CD@ZF can be attributed to its larger surface area, increased ROS production, and the suppression of photoinduced electron-hole pair recombination. Scheme 2 illustrates the redox mechanism through which ROS are generated by photoinduced electron-hole pairs and the adsorption of O2 and H2O on the photocatalytic surface[64,69].

To enhance photocatalytic performance, a large surface area, high adsorption capacity, and efficient charge separation are essential. According to the findings of this study, the hybrid nanocomposite, as-synthesized CD@ZF, demonstrated improved charge migration. In this composite, CD serves as an acceptor for both electrons and holes. The conduction band of the n-type ZF semiconductor facilitates the transfer of electrons to the CD material, while the valence band transfers holes. The integration of CD into the ZF composite slows the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs, resulting in improved photocatalytic efficiency for the CD@ZF binary composite compared to ZF nanoparticles. Various experimental results support the photodegradation mechanism of the binary photocatalyst, which will be discussed in detail later.

Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) is an effective technique for evaluating the efficiency of electron-hole pair separation in photocatalysts. Fig. 12 presents EIS results, revealing that the CD@ZF nanocomposite has a smaller arc radius compared to pure ZF, indicating its enhanced ability to inhibit electron-hole pair recombination and improve charge transfer at the interface[70]. Additionally, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the samples are shown in Fig. 8. CD@ZF demonstrates notable absorption in the visible region (while Tauc plots in Fig. 8b and 8c determine the energy gaps (Eg) for ZF and CD@ZF to be 2.11 eV and 1.95 eV, respectively, aligning with previous findings. The development of this new CD@ZF nanocomposite material significantly enhances photocatalytic performance by promoting more efficient charge separation and transfer. These results provide strong evidence supporting the material's improved photocatalytic activity.

Radical trapping experiments were used to identify the primary active species in the photocatalytic reaction. It is well-known that four key reactive species—superoxide radicals (O2), holes (h+), electrons (e−), and hydroxyl radicals (OH)—play crucial roles in the photocatalytic process. To determine the primary species involved in this process, trapping experiments were conducted. The photocatalytic activity of the CD@ZF nanocomposite in degrading Rhodamine B (Rh-B) and Methylene Blue (MB) was evaluated using ammonium oxalate (AO), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), benzoquinone (BQ), and isopropanol (IPA) as scavengers for h+, e−, O2, and OH, respectively, as shown in Fig. 13 a and 13b for RhB and MB, respectively. The results showed a significant reduction in photocatalytic degradation when BQ and IPA were added, indicating that O2 and OH are the main reactive species in the CD@ZF nanocomposite's photocatalytic process. In contrast, the presence of AO and NaNO3 had only a minor inhibitory effect on Rh-B and MB degradation, suggesting that h+ and e− have a minimal role in photocatalytic efficiency. These findings help describe the mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic activity in the CD@ZF nanocomposite.

The enhanced performance of nanocomposites is mainly attributed to the optimal positions of the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB), as well as the improved separation of charge carriers due to the contact interaction, as demonstrated in previous studies. The CB position of the ZF sample was determined using Mott-Schottky analysis (Fig. 14 ), which showed a flat band position of −0.67 V. The actual CB position for ZF was found to be −0.63 V, about 0.04 V more positive than the flat band potential[71,72]. The band gaps for ZF and CD@ZF, measured via UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, are 2.11 eV and 1.95 eV, respectively. From the band gap and CB data, the VB position for ZF is calculated to be 1.48 V. When ZF is exposed to visible light, electrons are excited from the VB to the CB, leaving holes (h+) in the VB, as explained by the band structure analysis of ZF. Scheme 2 presents a possible photoreaction mechanism, suggesting that under visible light, electrons are excited from the VB to the CB of ZF and are then transferred to the CD, leaving holes in the VB. The presence of CD aids in preventing electron-hole recombination, thereby enhancing the overall photocatalytic efficiency.

In photocatalysis technology, it is essential to recover and reuse the synthesized materials for treating wastewater. Fig. 15 illustrates the degradation percentages of RhB and MB dyes over irradiation time, based on five consecutive experiments using CD@ZF materials for dye photodegradation. In the first cycle, the catalyst achieved a degradation efficiency of 94.5 %. After five consecutive uses, the difference between the initial and final degradation efficiency was less than 5 %, indicating that the material remained stable and effective throughout the photocatalytic process. The catalyst's stability was further confirmed through XRD analysis after the photodegradation experiments (Fig. S1), which showed that the physical properties of the catalyst were unchanged, even after five cycles of photodegradation.

In conclusion, CD-modified ZF photocatalysts were successfully synthesized through a direct hydrothermal method. The incorporation of CDs into the ZF structure significantly enhanced the photocatalytic activity of the hybrid materials, particularly in the degradation of MB and RhB organic dyes under visible light. This enhancement resulted in 94.25 % degradation of MB within 70 min and 96.1 % degradation of RhB within 60 min. The improved performance is attributed to the crucial role of CDs in the ZF composite. Additionally, the CD@ZF nanocomposite demonstrated remarkable antibacterial activity against both Gram-positive (S. aureus) and Gram-negative (P. vulgaris) bacteria, making the synthesized material highly promising for both organic pollutant degradation and bacterial inhibition.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Tahmeena Akhter is a passionate researcher and a PhD scholar at the National Institute of Technology, Srinagar, with a focus on carbon-based nanocomposites. She holds an MSc in Chemistry from the esteemed University of Kashmir. Beyond her academic pursuits, Tahmeena is an avid observer of nature, a traveler, and a creative mind who finds joy in reading novels, writing, and drawing. Her dedication to scientific exploration and artistic expression reflects her multifaceted personality and commitment to excellence.

Dr. Mohammad Aslam is an accomplished academic and researcher, currently serving as an Assistant Professor at the National Institute of Technology, Srinagar. With expertise in Analytical Chemistry, Green Fuels, and Biorefinery, his work is dedicated to advancing sustainable energy solutions. He holds an MSc from the prestigious Aligarh Muslim University, an MTech from Birla Institute of Technology, Mesra, Ranchi, and earned his PhD from the National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar. Dr. Aslam's innovative research and dedication to eco-friendly technologies reflect his passion for addressing global energy challenges through science.
